---
title: "Module 9: Build and Deploy Agents"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## The API: Building Custom Chatbots {.section-divider}

## The API: Talking to an LLM from Code

An **API** lets your code communicate with an LLM service over the internet.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**What You Need**]{.card-title}

- An API key (authentication)
- The `anthropic` Python package for Claude
- The `openai` Python package for OpenAI and many other models
- A model name (e.g., `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`)
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Getting Your Key**]{.card-title}

- Go to `console.anthropic.com`
- Create an API key under Settings
- Add credit ($5 is plenty for the course)
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
Your Claude Pro subscription covers Claude.ai and Claude Code. The API is a separate product with pay-per-use pricing.
:::

## The Model Landscape: OpenRouter

[OpenRouter](https://openrouter.ai/models) is a marketplace that routes API calls to hundreds of models from dozens of providers --- [one account, one API key, any model]{.amber}.

- **One key for everything** --- OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Mistral, and more; swap the model name without changing providers or billing accounts
- **Billing flows through OpenRouter** --- providers bill OpenRouter; OpenRouter bills you. No separate OpenAI or Anthropic accounts needed.
- **Pricing transparency** --- cost per million input/output tokens shown for every model; compare before committing

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
Visit [openrouter.ai/models](https://openrouter.ai/models) to see the full model landscape and pricing.
:::

## A Single API Call

:::{.info-box}
[**Calling the Anthropic API**]{.box-title}

```python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=API_KEY)

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": prompt}
    ]
)
answer = response.content[0].text
```
:::

## Conversation History

:::{.info-box}
[**Messages Are a List of Dictionaries**]{.box-title}

```python
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system="You are a finance tutor...",
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is a P/E ratio?"},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "A P/E ratio is..."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "How do I interpret it?"}
    ]
)
```
:::

- Each API call is independent --- LLM has no memory
- You must send the [entire conversation history]{.amber} each time
- The **system prompt** defines the agent's behavior and available tools

## How a Web App Works

Every web app has two parts that communicate over the internet.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Frontend**]{.card-title}

- Runs in the [user's browser]{.amber}
- HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
- Sends requests to the backend and displays results
- Cannot safely hold API keys --- source is visible to anyone
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Backend**]{.card-title}

- Runs on [a server]{.amber} (your laptop counts during development)
- Python, Node.js, or any language
- Holds secrets (API keys, database credentials)
- Receives requests from the frontend, does the work, returns results
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
For a chatbot: browser sends the user's message → backend calls the LLM API → backend returns the reply → browser displays it.
:::

## FastAPI {.section-divider}

## Why FastAPI?

[FastAPI](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com) is a modern Python framework for building web backends.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Why FastAPI**]{.card-title}

- Async-native --- handles many requests concurrently
- Minimal boilerplate --- a working API in under 20 lines
- Auto-generated interactive docs at `/docs`
- Industry standard for Python APIs
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**For a Chatbot Backend**]{.card-title}

- One endpoint receives the user's message and conversation history
- Backend calls the Anthropic API with your [server-side API key]{.amber}
- Returns the assistant's reply to the browser
- Key never touches the frontend
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
FastAPI is what sits between your chat interface and the LLM --- it keeps credentials secure and handles the API calls.
:::

## When Pyodide Isn't Enough

Pyodide HTML apps are self-contained and shareable, but they have limitations.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Pyodide: Client-Side Only**]{.card-title}

- Cannot connect to live databases
- No persistent storage between sessions
- API keys must be embedded (insecure)
- Single user, single device
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**FastAPI: Server-Side Power**]{.card-title}

- Live database connections
- Persistent storage
- Secure API key management via environment variables
- Multi-user access controls
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
FastAPI gives you a real backend server where Python runs on the machine hosting the app.
:::

## A Minimal FastAPI App

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/")
def read_root():
    return {"message": "Hello World"}

@app.get("/items/{item_id}")
def read_item(item_id: int, q: str = None):
    return {"item_id": item_id, "q": q}
```

Run locally: `uvicorn main:app --reload`

The app is live at `http://localhost:8000` with automatic docs at `/docs`.

## Exercise: Build a FastAPI Chatbot

Ask Claude Code:

*"Build a chatbot web app using FastAPI and the Anthropic API. The frontend should be a simple HTML page with a text input and a chat window. The backend should call Claude and maintain conversation history. Store the API key in a `.env` file."*

Then:

1. Claude Code creates the backend (`main.py`) and frontend (`index.html`)
2. Run the server: `uvicorn main:app --reload`
3. Open `http://localhost:8000` in your browser and chat

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
This is the same architecture used by every production chatbot. [Claude Code writes all the code]{.amber} --- your job is to test and refine it.
:::

## Try These System Prompts

Edit the system prompt in your chatbot and ask the same question each time. See how the response changes.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Haiku only**]{.card-title}

*"Respond to every message as a haiku (5-7-5 syllables), no matter how complex the question."*
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Pirate**]{.card-title}

*"You are a pirate. Respond to every message in pirate dialect."*
:::
:::

:::{.two-cards style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Medieval knight**]{.card-title}

*"You are a medieval knight. Respond to every message as if explaining it to a peasant in the 14th century."*
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Sports announcer**]{.card-title}

*"You are a sports announcer. Describe everything the user says as if it's the most exciting play in history."*
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
Then try a [finance-relevant system prompt]{.amber}: *"You are a CFO. Answer every question in terms of its impact on cash flow and shareholder value."*
:::

## API Keys: Never in Code

When calling external APIs like Anthropic, your key must never be in your code or repo.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**User-Supplied Key (Client-Side)**]{.card-title}

- User enters their key in a web form
- Key travels with each request
- Right when: each user has their own account
- Example: OpenAI's ChatGPT interface
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Server-Side Key (Environment Variable)**]{.card-title}

- Key set on the server, never seen by users
- One key serves all users
- Right when: you operate the service
- Example: Your deployed app calling Anthropic
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
For class exercises, user-supplied keys are simpler: no deployment setup needed.
:::

## In-Class Exercise: Investment Summary Tool {.section-divider}

## Exercise: Investment Summary Tool

Ask Claude Code:

*"Create a FastAPI app with two endpoints:
1. `GET /` serves an HTML form where the user enters a stock ticker and their Anthropic API key
2. `POST /analyze` accepts the ticker and API key, calls the Anthropic API asking for an investment summary, and returns the AI-generated analysis

Use a user-supplied API key, not an environment variable."*

**What this teaches:**
- Making HTTP requests from FastAPI to external APIs
- Handling API keys securely (user-supplied pattern)
- Serving HTML from FastAPI alongside JSON endpoints

## The Agent Pattern {.section-divider}

## Agent Loop Pseudocode

:::{.info-box}
[**The Agent's Decision Loop**]{.box-title}

```python
while not done:
    response = call_llm(messages, system_prompt, tools)

    if response.has_tool_call:
        result = execute_tool(response.tool_call)
        messages.append(tool_result)
    elif response.is_final:
        done = True
        return response.content
```
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em; text-align:center;"}
[The agent is part LLM intelligence, part traditional programming.]{.amber}
:::

## Claude Code as an Agent Harness

The pseudocode shows the logic. A **harness** is the infrastructure that actually runs that loop --- and controls what the agent is allowed to do.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Runtime Infrastructure**]{.card-title}

- Maintains conversation state across turns
- Executes tool calls and captures results
- Handles errors so the agent can recover
- Enforces iteration and cost limits
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Control and Safety**]{.card-title}

- Permission system: which tools are allowed
- Human-in-the-loop approval gates
- Hooks: custom checks before/after actions
- Audit log of every action taken
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
Claude Code is a complete harness --- you supply the task; it handles the loop, tools, and guardrails.
:::

## The Real Agent Loop

:::{.info-box}
[**A Production-Grade Loop**]{.box-title}

```python
iteration = 0
while iteration < max_turns:
    response = call_llm(messages, system_prompt, tools)
    messages.append(response)             # Maintain full history

    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        return response.text              # Task complete

    for tool_call in response.tool_uses:
        if needs_approval(tool_call):     # Human-in-the-loop
            if not ask_user(tool_call):
                break                     # User blocked the action
        try:
            result = execute(tool_call)
        except Exception as e:
            result = f"Error: {e}"        # Agent sees error and recovers
        messages.append(tool_result(result))

    iteration += 1
raise MaxIterationsError("Did not complete")
```
:::

## Claude Code's Four Harness Layers

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**CLAUDE.md**]{.card-title}

Injected as the system prompt. Defines the agent's persona, project context, and behavioral rules before any user message is sent.
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Permissions**]{.card-title}

Controls which tools and file paths the agent can access. Separate allow/deny lists for bash commands, file writes, and network calls.
:::
:::

:::{.two-cards style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Hooks**]{.card-title}

Shell commands that fire automatically at agent lifecycle events: before a tool runs, after it completes, or when the agent stops.
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Iteration Limits**]{.card-title}

`--max-turns` caps how many steps the agent takes. Prevents runaway loops and controls API spend.
:::
:::

## Replacing Dashboards {.section-divider}

## The Dashboard Trap

Organizations spend millions building dashboards. When a new question arises, the cycle restarts: requirements --> design --> build --> deploy.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-dark}
[The Typical Dashboard Lifecycle]{.card-title}

1. Business user requests a report
2. Team builds queries, charts, and deploys
3. User asks a follow-up --- [back to step 1]{.amber}
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[The Cost]{.card-title}

- **Time**: Weeks to months per dashboard
- **Money**: BI licenses, engineering hours, maintenance
- **Rigidity**: Fixed views of fixed data

Gartner: only **20%** of analytic insights deliver business outcomes.
:::
:::

## The Fundamental Problem

Dashboards answer **pre-defined questions**. But the most valuable analysis comes from **ad-hoc questions** that arise in the moment.

- "What happened to margins in the Southeast last quarter?"
- "Show me our top 10 customers by growth rate, excluding one-time orders"
- "Compare Q3 headcount vs. budget by department, and flag anyone over 110%"

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
These are simple questions. Getting answers shouldn't require a development cycle.
:::

## The Shift: From Dashboards to Conversations

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Traditional Dashboard**

- Click filters and select dates to query
- Answers take minutes to weeks
- Follow-ups require a new dashboard request
::::

::::{.column}
**Natural Language AI**

- Ask in plain English
- Answers in seconds
- Follow-ups are the next sentence
::::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:1em; text-align:center;"}
The dashboard was a *workaround* for the fact that databases don't speak English. Now they do.
:::

## What This Looks Like in Practice

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
:::{.info-box}
[**The Conversation**]{.box-title}

- *"Show me monthly revenue by product line for 2025"*
- AI: writes SQL, produces grouped bar chart
- *"Break out Enterprise by region"*
- AI: refines query, updates chart
:::
::::

::::{.column}
**What the User Needed to Know**

- What questions to ask
- Whether the answers make sense
- [Nothing else]{.amber}

**Behind the Scenes**

- 4 different SQL queries written
- 3 visualizations produced
- Derived metrics calculated
::::
:::

## The Database Agent Pattern

The most powerful dashboard replacement: an AI agent connected to your database.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**How to Build It**]{.card-title}

1. Connect database via MCP or file upload
2. Give AI the schema: table names, columns, relationships
3. Describe the business context
4. Start asking questions
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**What the Agent Can Do**]{.card-title}

- Write and execute SQL queries
- Compute derived metrics (growth rates, ratios)
- Generate charts and export to Excel or PowerPoint
:::
:::

## Replacing Dashboards: FP&A and Treasury

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**FP&A**]{.card-title}

- *"Budget vs. actual for Q3, decompose the variance into volume, price, and cost drivers"*
- *"Rolling 12-month revenue forecast with confidence bands"*
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Treasury & Risk**]{.card-title}

- *"Cash position over the next 90 days using AR/AP forecasts"*
- *"VaR by desk for the last 30 days, flag breaches"*
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
Each of these would take a BI team **days to build**. With a database agent, they take [seconds]{.amber}.
:::

## Replacing Dashboards: Portfolio and Executive

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Portfolio Management**]{.card-title}

- *"Sector allocation vs. benchmark with active weights"*
- *"Performance attribution --- allocation vs. selection"*
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Executive Reporting**]{.card-title}

- *"One-page executive summary with KPIs and trends"*
- *"Board deck from this quarter's financials --- 5 slides max"*
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
The AI doesn't replace the analyst's judgment --- it replaces the [mechanical work]{.amber} of pulling data and building charts.
:::

## The Weekly Ops Review --- Before and After

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-dark}
[Before: The Dashboard Era]{.card-title}

1. Data team pulls exports and builds slides (6+ hrs)
2. Manager reviews and revises (3 hrs)
3. VP asks a question --- "We'll get back to you next week"

**Total: 9+ hours per week**
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[After: Natural Language AI]{.card-title}

1. AI agent generates ops review (3 min)
2. Manager reviews and iterates in chat
3. VP asks a question --- [AI answers in 10 seconds]{.amber}

**Total: 15 minutes + live Q&A**
:::
:::

## The Reporting Pipeline {.section-divider}

## Five Steps from Query to Report

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '28px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 100, 'rankSpacing': 140, 'padding': 28, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart LR
  A["<b>Database<br/>Query</b>"] --> B["<b>Transform<br/>& Compute</b>"]
  B --> C["<b>Charts &<br/>Tables</b>"]
  C --> D["<b>AI<br/>Narrative</b>"]
  D --> E["<b>PowerPoint<br/>Output</b>"]

  style A fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style B fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style C fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style D fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style E fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
```

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em; text-align:center;"}
From raw data to polished deck --- [no copy-paste, no formatting, no manual writing.]{.amber}
:::

## Building It with Your Coding Agent

You do not need to write this by hand. Give your coding agent a single prompt:

:::{.info-box}
*"Build an app that connects to a database. When the user selects a report and clicks Generate, run the SQL query, create a chart, write a three-sentence executive summary, and assemble a three-slide PowerPoint (title, chart, findings). Show the chart and summary on screen with a download button for the PowerPoint file."*
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
Your job is not to write the code. Your job is to [test the result, refine the prompt, and iterate]{.amber} until the output meets your standards.
:::

## Building the Pipeline with Streamlit

**Streamlit** turns a Python script into a web app in minutes. Combined with an AI pipeline, it becomes a [self-service reporting tool]{.amber}.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**User Interface**]{.card-title}

- Dropdown: select time period, department, or metric
- Button: "Generate Report"
- Download: auto-generated `.pptx` file
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Behind the Scenes**]{.card-title}

- Query database for selected parameters
- Compute metrics and build charts
- Send chart data to LLM for narrative
- Assemble PowerPoint with `python-pptx`
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
The user clicks one button and gets a polished deck. [No analyst needed.]{.amber}
:::

## Example: Monthly Ops Review Pipeline

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**What the Pipeline Produces**

1. Title slide, KPI summary table, and trend charts
2. Variance waterfall (budget vs. actual)
3. AI-generated executive summary
::::

::::{.column}
**Time Comparison**

- **Manual**: 6--8 hours per month (data pull, Excel, copy-paste into PowerPoint, write narrative)
- **Pipeline**: [30 seconds]{.amber} per run
- **ROI**: First month pays for the build time
::::
:::

## Automated PowerPoint with python-pptx

`python-pptx` is a Python library for creating and editing PowerPoint files programmatically.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**What It Can Do**]{.card-title}

- Create slides from templates
- Insert charts, tables, and images
- Apply corporate formatting (fonts, colors, logos)
- Populate placeholders with live data
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**The AI Advantage**]{.card-title}

- AI writes the `python-pptx` code for you
- Tell Claude: "Create a 5-slide deck from this data with a waterfall chart on slide 3"
- AI handles layout, formatting, and data binding
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
[You describe the deck; AI builds the automation.]{.amber} The pipeline runs unattended after that.
:::

## Orchestration {.section-divider}

## The Orchestration Layer

The agent's control logic can route different tasks to different models and prompts.

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Different Prompts**]{.card-title}

- SQL generation --> database schema prompt
- Python analysis --> data science prompt
- Each task gets specialized instructions
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Different Models**]{.card-title}

- Simple classification --> fast, cheap model
- Complex reasoning --> powerful model
- Cost and speed optimization
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
Sub-agents: dispatch specialized workers for parallel tasks. This is what Cowork does with its parallel VMs.
:::

## Sandboxed Execution {.section-divider}

## Development vs. Production

:::{.two-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Development (Your Laptop)**]{.card-title}

- Agent runs in your environment
- Full access to your files
- Fine for prototyping
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Production**]{.card-title}

- Agent runs in a [container]{.amber} (Docker)
- Isolated, disposable environment
- Read-only database access
:::
:::

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
A container is a disposable, isolated computing environment. A bug in AI-generated code cannot affect your other files, your database, or your network.
:::

## The Sandbox Pattern

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 16
%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': {'fontSize': '28px'}, 'flowchart': {'nodeSpacing': 80, 'rankSpacing': 120, 'padding': 24, 'useMaxWidth': true}}}%%
flowchart LR
  U["<b>User</b>"] --> A["<b>Agent</b>"]
  A --> S["<b>Sandboxed<br>Code</b>"]
  S <--> DB["<b>Database<br>(read-only)</b>"]
  S --> R["<b>Report</b>"]
  R --> U

  style U fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style A fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style S fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style DB fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
  style R fill:#eff6ff,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a,font-size:28px,padding:24px
```

- Agent plans and orchestrates; code runs in a [sandboxed container]{.amber}
- Container queries the database through a read-only connection
- Finished report flows back to the user

## From Prototype to Production

The reporting app on your laptop is a prototype. Moving it to production adds **infrastructure**, not intelligence.

- **Authentication**: SSO so only authorized users can generate reports
- **Scheduling**: weekly reports generated automatically
- **Logging**: every query and LLM call recorded for compliance
- **Read-only credentials**: the agent cannot modify data

:::{.explainer style="margin-top:0.8em;"}
The pipeline you prototyped is the same artifact that powers the production tool. IT wraps it in infrastructure; [you provide the domain knowledge and the prompt]{.amber}.
:::

## Deploying on Railway {.section-divider}

## Deployment Options

**Where do production apps actually run?**

- **Corporate intranet / IT-managed** — most common in practice; IT provides auth, logging, security compliance
- **PaaS (Railway, Render, Koyeb)** — self-service, minimal setup; ideal for prototypes and course projects
- **IaaS (AWS, Azure, GCP)** — full control, more complexity; where large-scale apps live

For this course: **Railway** — simplest path from `git push` to live URL.

## Deploying with Railway

**Three steps to production:**

1. **Connect GitHub repo** — one-click OAuth
2. **Railway detects FastAPI** — automatically configures Python environment
3. **Every `git push` redeploys** — watch the dashboard for live status

The Railway dashboard shows:
- Live URL (updates automatically)
- Build and runtime logs
- Environment variables (for API keys)
- Deploy history

## Demo: Deploy the Simple App

**Instructor walkthrough:**

1. Push the loan payment FastAPI app to GitHub
2. Connect repo to Railway
3. Watch the build process in the Railway dashboard
4. Click the live URL: `https://your-app.railway.app/loan_payment?principal=300000&rate=0.065&years=30`

**Students see:** the same app they ran locally, now live on the internet.

## Demo: Deploy with Environment Variables

**Instructor walkthrough:**

1. Modify the investment summary app to use an environment variable:
   ```python
   import os
   api_key = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")
   ```

2. Deploy to Railway
3. In Railway dashboard → Variables, add `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
4. The app works without users supplying their own key

:::{.explainer style="text-align:center;"}
The API key lives only on Railway's servers --- never in code, never in GitHub, never seen by users.
:::

## Exercises {.section-divider}

## Exercise 1: Simple FastAPI App

Ask Claude Code:

*"Create a FastAPI app with a single endpoint `/loan_payment` that accepts `principal`, `rate`, and `years` as query parameters and returns the monthly payment amount. Include instructions for running it locally."*

**Steps:**
1. Create a new folder for the project
2. Ask Claude to build the FastAPI app
3. Run `uvicorn main:app --reload`
4. Test the endpoint in your browser: `http://localhost:8000/loan_payment?principal=300000&rate=0.065&years=30`

## Exercise 2: Investment Summary with User API Key

Build a FastAPI app that takes a ticker and Anthropic API key from a web form, calls the Anthropic API, and displays an investment summary.

## Exercise 3: Multi-Step Agent Workflow

Give Claude Code a single compound instruction that requires at least three steps.

**Example:** *"Fetch Apple's quarterly revenue from the Rice Data Portal. Create a bar chart of the last 8 quarters. Write a one-paragraph executive summary. Save the chart and summary to a reports folder."*

- Observe the agent loop --- how many distinct tool calls does the agent make?
- Does it check its own work?
- If the output is not right, refine your prompt and try again

## Exercise 4: Workflow Decomposition

Consider the task: *"Prepare the quarterly business review for the CEO."*

1. List the 5--7 steps a human analyst would take to complete this task
2. For each step, identify what **tool** the agent would use and what **data** it would need
3. Mark which steps need [human approval]{.amber} before the agent continues
4. Write a single prompt that describes the full workflow for an agent

## Exercise 5: Deploy to Railway

Deploy either app to Railway and submit the live URL.

## Exercise 6: Streamlit App with the Anthropic API

1. Get an API key from `console.anthropic.com`
2. Ask Claude Code to build a Streamlit app that:
   - Takes a company ticker from the user
   - Sends a prompt to the Anthropic API asking for an investment summary
   - Displays the AI-generated summary on screen
3. Extend: add a system prompt with specific analysis criteria (valuation, growth, risks)

## Later Course Integration: RAG with FastAPI

In Module 11 (RAG), a capstone exercise combines all concepts:

*"Build a FastAPI app where the user pastes a document and asks a question. The app chunks the document, embeds it with OpenAI, stores in ChromaDB, retrieves relevant chunks, and generates an answer via Anthropic's API. The user supplies their own API keys."*

This ties together: FastAPI, external APIs, vector databases, and RAG --- all in one deployable application.
